**Walter Zenga** [Cavaliere OMRI](/wiki/Order_of_Merit_of_the_Italian_Republic "Order of Merit of the Italian Republic") (Italian pronunciation: [[ˈvalter ˈdzeŋɡa, - ˈdzɛŋ-]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); born 28 April 1960) is an Italian professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") [manager](/wiki/Manager_(association_football) "Manager (association football)") and former player, currently club manager at [Serie C](/wiki/Serie_C "Serie C") club [Siracusa](/wiki/ASD_Siracusa_Calcio_1924 "ASD Siracusa Calcio 1924").

He is regarded as one of the greatest goalkeepers of his generation,[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) and one of Italy's best keepers ever.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

He was a long-time [goalkeeper](/wiki/Goalkeeper_(association_football) "Goalkeeper (association football)") for [Inter Milan](/wiki/Inter_Milan "Inter Milan") and the [Italy national team](/wiki/Italy_national_football_team "Italy national football team"). During his playing career, Zenga was part of the Italian squad that finished fourth at the [1984 Olympics](/wiki/1984_Summer_Olympics "1984 Summer Olympics") in Los Angeles and was the starting goalkeeper for the Italian team that finished third in the [1990 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1990_FIFA_World_Cup "1990 FIFA World Cup") tournament held in Italy, keeping a World Cup record unbeaten streak.[[7]](#cite_note-alt-7) A three-time winner of the [IFFHS World's Best Goalkeeper](/wiki/IFFHS_World%27s_Best_Goalkeeper "IFFHS World's Best Goalkeeper") Award, Zenga is regarded by pundits as one of the best goalkeepers of all time,[[8]](#cite_note-8) and in 2013 was voted the eighth best goalkeeper of the past quarter-century by [IFFHS](/wiki/IFFHS "IFFHS").[[9]](#cite_note-9) In 2000, he also placed 20th in the *World Keeper of the Century* Elections by the same organisation.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

After retiring as a player, Zenga briefly became an actor in an Italian [soap opera](/wiki/Soap_opera "Soap opera") and also a [pundit](/wiki/Color_commentator "Color commentator") on Italian TV. Since 1998 he has worked as a [head coach](/wiki/Manager_(association_football) "Manager (association football)") and managed clubs in the United States, Italy, Turkey, Romania, Serbia, Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates and England.

## Club career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walter_Zenga&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Club career")]

Zenga joined [Inter Milan](/wiki/Inter_Milan "Inter Milan") in 1982, after starting his professional career in 1978 in the lower divisions of Italian football (his first team was [Salernitana](/wiki/Salernitana_Calcio_1919 "Salernitana Calcio 1919") in [Serie C1](/wiki/Lega_Pro_Prima_Divisione "Lega Pro Prima Divisione"), and he also played for [Savona](/wiki/Savona_1907_F.B.C. "Savona 1907 F.B.C.") and [Sambenedettese](/wiki/S.S._Sambenedettese_Calcio "S.S. Sambenedettese Calcio")). Initially, (in the [1982–83](/wiki/1982%E2%80%9383_Serie_A "1982–83 Serie A") season) he was the substitute of [Ivano Bordon](/wiki/Ivano_Bordon "Ivano Bordon"), who was one of the top Italian goalkeepers of his era, as he had been [Dino Zoff](/wiki/Dino_Zoff "Dino Zoff")'s reserve in the [1982 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1982_FIFA_World_Cup "1982 FIFA World Cup"). However, Zenga played Inter's matches in the [Coppa Italia](/wiki/Coppa_Italia "Coppa Italia"), impressing enough that the club decided not to buy another goalkeeper after Bordon's decision to move to [Sampdoria](/wiki/U.C._Sampdoria "U.C. Sampdoria") during the summer of 1983. Zenga became Inter's starting goalkeeper in the [1983–84](/wiki/1983%E2%80%9384_Serie_A "1983–84 Serie A") season, where he conceded only 23 goals, better than any other goalkeeper in that season.[[11]](#cite_note-storiedicalcio-11)

The next season would prove to be bittersweet for Zenga: although he continued to play excellently, he did not manage to win any trophies. In Italy, Inter was the main rival of [Hellas Verona](/wiki/Hellas_Verona_F.C. "Hellas Verona F.C.") who won the first and, to this day, only [*Scudetto*](/wiki/List_of_Italian_football_champions "List of Italian football champions") of its history in 1985, while in Europe he had to suffer two bitter and quite controversial defeats at the hands of Spanish giants [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid_C.F. "Real Madrid C.F."), both times in the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup") semi-finals. However, personal success was growing: he became a fan favourite due to his qualities and his love for the team, his fame was now nationwide thanks to his larger than life personality and he quickly established himself as one of the premier goalkeepers of the country, which led to him being called up to Italy's squad for the 1986 World Cup.[[11]](#cite_note-storiedicalcio-11)

Apart from enjoying the selection for a World Cup, the summer of 1986 proved to be important for Zenga also at club level. In fact, Inter signed [Giovanni Trapattoni](/wiki/Giovanni_Trapattoni "Giovanni Trapattoni"), who left [Juventus](/wiki/Juventus_FC "Juventus FC") after a highly successful 10-year stint, to manage the team. Meanwhile, the trio formed by Zenga, [Giuseppe Bergomi](/wiki/Giuseppe_Bergomi "Giuseppe Bergomi") and [Riccardo Ferri](/wiki/Riccardo_Ferri "Riccardo Ferri") (who respectively occupied the positions of goalkeeper, right-sided full-back, and man-marking centre-back/stopper) was becoming the cornerstone of the team and of the Italian team also. In the [1986–87](/wiki/1986%E2%80%9387_Serie_A "1986–87 Serie A") season. Inter closely fought [Napoli](/wiki/S.S.C._Napoli "S.S.C. Napoli") for the *Scudetto*, finishing third despite a series of injuries which plagued the team in the final weeks of the season (among others, [Marco Tardelli](/wiki/Marco_Tardelli "Marco Tardelli"), [Alessandro Altobelli](/wiki/Alessandro_Altobelli "Alessandro Altobelli") and [Karl-Heinz Rummenigge](/wiki/Karl-Heinz_Rummenigge "Karl-Heinz Rummenigge") had to watch the final matches from the bench). However, Zenga imposed himself as the best goalkeeper in Italy, finishing the 30 matches-long season conceding only 17 goals and by being picked by new Italy's manager [Azeglio Vicini](/wiki/Azeglio_Vicini "Azeglio Vicini") as the starter in the goalkeeping position.[[11]](#cite_note-storiedicalcio-11)

The next season would prove to be disappointing for Inter and Zenga: the team struggled all the season, due to lack of compatibility between the two main forwards (team's captain Altobelli and the newly acquired [Aldo Serena](/wiki/Aldo_Serena "Aldo Serena")) and between the two offensive midfielders [Gianfranco Matteoli](/wiki/Gianfranco_Matteoli "Gianfranco Matteoli") and the Belgian [Vincenzo Scifo](/wiki/Vincenzo_Scifo "Vincenzo Scifo"). Plus Zenga, dissatisfied with the way the club was managed, decided to leave Inter and join the then dominant Napoli. However, the move did not materialize and Zenga remained with Inter. The highlight of the season for Zenga was the participation in the [1988 UEFA European Championships](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1988 "UEFA Euro 1988") with Italy.[[11]](#cite_note-storiedicalcio-11)[[12]](#cite_note-itv-12)

However, the next season would prove to be one of the best for Inter and Zenga. The team, reinvigorated by the acquisitions of the young Italians [Alessandro Bianchi](/wiki/Alessandro_Bianchi_(footballer_born_1966) "Alessandro Bianchi (footballer born 1966)") and [Nicola Berti](/wiki/Nicola_Berti "Nicola Berti"), the Germans [Andreas Brehme](/wiki/Andreas_Brehme "Andreas Brehme") and [Lothar Matthäus](/wiki/Lothar_Matth%C3%A4us "Lothar Matthäus") from [Bayern Munich](/wiki/FC_Bayern_Munich "FC Bayern Munich") and the Argentine [Ramón Díaz](/wiki/Ram%C3%B3n_D%C3%ADaz "Ramón Díaz") dominated the season, winning the league title with a record haul of 58 points and breaking several other records during the year. Such a performance is even more impressive if the whole quality of the tournament is taken in consideration: in second position there was the [Diego Maradona](/wiki/Diego_Maradona "Diego Maradona")-led Napoli and in third position the star-studded and future European champion [Milan](/wiki/A.C._Milan "A.C. Milan"). Zenga ended the season conceding only 19 goals, the best goalkeeper again in that respect.[[11]](#cite_note-storiedicalcio-11)

The [1989–90](/wiki/1989%E2%80%9390_Serie_A "1989–90 Serie A") and [1990–91](/wiki/1990%E2%80%9391_Serie_A "1990–91 Serie A") seasons proved to be bittersweet for Inter: although the team remained a title contender, it didn't manage to take another success on home soil, except for the victory in the [Supercoppa Italiana](/wiki/Supercoppa_Italiana "Supercoppa Italiana") played in November 1989 against Sampdoria. The 1991 season turned out to be a close fight between Inter and Sampdoria, with the title decided in a match played in Milan, which Inter would lose 0–2 allowing [Gianluca Vialli](/wiki/Gianluca_Vialli "Gianluca Vialli") and [Roberto Mancini](/wiki/Roberto_Mancini "Roberto Mancini") to win the league title. However, Inter won the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/1990%E2%80%9391_UEFA_Cup "1990–91 UEFA Cup") that year, defeating, among the others, [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C."), [Atalanta](/wiki/Atalanta_B.C. "Atalanta B.C.") and [Sporting CP](/wiki/Sporting_Clube_de_Portugal "Sporting Clube de Portugal") on the road to the final against [Roma](/wiki/A.S._Roma "A.S. Roma"). Inter won the first match 2–0 and lost only 1–0 in Rome, achieving the first European success since the 1960s. After that match, manager Giovanni Trapattoni left the team, as he decided to return as coach of Juventus.[[11]](#cite_note-storiedicalcio-11)

On a personal scale, Zenga experienced in these seasons the peak of his career. For three consecutive years (1989–1991) he was nominated by [IFFHS](/wiki/IFFHS "IFFHS") the best goalkeeper in the world, ahead of goalkeepers like [Michel Preud'homme](/wiki/Michel_Preud%27homme "Michel Preud'homme"), [Rinat Dasaev](/wiki/Rinat_Dasaev "Rinat Dasaev") and [Andoni Zubizarreta](/wiki/Andoni_Zubizarreta "Andoni Zubizarreta").[[7]](#cite_note-alt-7) Zenga was at his best between the posts, as his great explosiveness and sharp reflexes enabled him to make great and spectacular saves. Not known for being a great penalty saver (frequently dropping down to the ground in the middle of the goal), in his career he did however save [penalty kicks](/wiki/Penalty_kick_(association_football) "Penalty kick (association football)") from [Roberto Baggio](/wiki/Roberto_Baggio "Roberto Baggio"), [Michel Platini](/wiki/Michel_Platini "Michel Platini") and [Paul Merson](/wiki/Paul_Merson "Paul Merson").[[11]](#cite_note-storiedicalcio-11)

Zenga continued to play for Inter until 1994, winning the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League") in 1991 and [1994](/wiki/1993%E2%80%9394_UEFA_Cup "1993–94 UEFA Cup"), his last season with the club.[[11]](#cite_note-storiedicalcio-11)

In 1994, Zenga transferred to [Sampdoria](/wiki/U.C._Sampdoria "U.C. Sampdoria"),[[13]](#cite_note-13) and then to [Padova](/wiki/Calcio_Padova "Calcio Padova") two years later.

### New England Revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walter_Zenga&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: New England Revolution")]

On March 4, 1997, [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer") allocated Zenga to the [New England Revolution](/wiki/New_England_Revolution "New England Revolution").[[2]](#cite_note-New_England_Revolution_Club_History-2) He made his Revolution debut in the [1997 season](/wiki/1997_New_England_Revolution_season "1997 New England Revolution season") opener on March 29, a 1–0 victory over the [Dallas Burn](/wiki/Dallas_Burn "Dallas Burn").[[14]](#cite_note-2024_Media_Guide-14) He made his home debut on April 20, in a 2–1 victory over the [Tampa Bay Mutiny](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Mutiny "Tampa Bay Mutiny").[[14]](#cite_note-2024_Media_Guide-14) During a game versus the Tampa Bay Mutiny, he [celebrated a goal](/wiki/Goal_celebration "Goal celebration") by running to the sidelines and making out with his girlfriend, as the Mutiny barely missed the open net straight from the kickoff.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In June On July 20, the club placed Zenga on injured reserve following a knee injury.[[2]](#cite_note-New_England_Revolution_Club_History-2) He would fly to Italy following the Revolution's 4–2 victory over Tampa Bay on July 18 for arthroscopic surgery.[[15]](#cite_note-15) [Scott Coufal](/wiki/Scott_Coufal "Scott Coufal") and [Jeff Causey](/wiki/Jeff_Causey "Jeff Causey") would fill in for Zenga until his return to net on September 12.[[14]](#cite_note-2024_Media_Guide-14) Zenga was awarded [MLS Player of the Month](/wiki/MLS_Player_of_the_Month "MLS Player of the Month") honors for the month of September, the first Revolution player to ever win the award.[[14]](#cite_note-2024_Media_Guide-14) Despite missing time, Zenga was a finalist for the [1997](/wiki/1997_Major_League_Soccer_season "1997 Major League Soccer season") [MLS Goalkeeper of the Year Award](/wiki/MLS_Goalkeeper_of_the_Year_Award "MLS Goalkeeper of the Year Award"). He additionally represented the Revolution in the [1997 MLS All-Star Game](/wiki/1997_MLS_All-Star_Game "1997 MLS All-Star Game"), and won the team's Most Valuable Player Award.[[14]](#cite_note-2024_Media_Guide-14) Prior to the All-Star game, at a fan event in [Federal Hill, Providence, Rhode Island](/wiki/Federal_Hill,_Providence,_Rhode_Island "Federal Hill, Providence, Rhode Island"), then-Providence Mayor [Buddy Cianci](/wiki/Buddy_Cianci "Buddy Cianci") presented Zenga with a bottle of his personal marinara sauce, "Mayor’s Own Marinara Sauce."[[16]](#cite_note-16) The Revolution supporter's group [the Midnight Riders](/wiki/Midnight_Riders_(MLS_supporters_association) "Midnight Riders (MLS supporters association)") named Zenga their 1997 "Man of the Year."[[17]](#cite_note-Man_of_the_Year_Award-17)

On January 15, 1998, Zenga announced his retirement.[[2]](#cite_note-New_England_Revolution_Club_History-2) He had led the Revolution to their first playoff berth in team history,[[18]](#cite_note-Zenga_Player_Coach-18) and he finished the 1997 season with the second best goals-against average (1.27), second highest win total (15), and best league shootout record, allowing only 12 goals in 41 attempts.[[19]](#cite_note-Zenga_Announces_Retirement-19) Despite his retirement, Zenga would continue his affiliation with the club as a part-time European scout.[[19]](#cite_note-Zenga_Announces_Retirement-19)

Zenga departed the Revolution to pursue an acting career (he and his girlfriend starred in an Italian [soap opera](/wiki/Soap_opera "Soap opera")),[[19]](#cite_note-Zenga_Announces_Retirement-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) however he would return on August 24, 1998, being named Revolution head coach after the resignation of [Thomas Rongen](/wiki/Thomas_Rongen "Thomas Rongen") on the same day.[[2]](#cite_note-New_England_Revolution_Club_History-2) Zenga led the Revolution to a 3–3 record in the final 6 matches of the [1998 New England Revolution season](/wiki/1998_New_England_Revolution_season "1998 New England Revolution season").[[18]](#cite_note-Zenga_Player_Coach-18)

Zenga stipulated that he would only return to the Revolution for the [1999 season](/wiki/1999_New_England_Revolution_season "1999 New England Revolution season") if he could play as well as coach, and he became the Revolution's (and league's) first-ever player/coach on October 28, 1998.[[18]](#cite_note-Zenga_Player_Coach-18)[[2]](#cite_note-New_England_Revolution_Club_History-2) As a player-[manager](/wiki/Coach_(sport) "Coach (sport)"), Zenga led the Revolution to a 10–20 record.[[11]](#cite_note-storiedicalcio-11) After issuing an ultimatum, demanding contract extension discussions begin prior to the season's end, Zenga was relieved of his duties on September 30, with interim manager [Steve Nicol](/wiki/Steve_Nicol "Steve Nicol") being brought in to conclude the season.[[2]](#cite_note-New_England_Revolution_Club_History-2)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## International career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walter_Zenga&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: International career")]

Zenga was capped 58 times for the [Italy national football team](/wiki/Italy_national_football_team "Italy national football team") at senior level between 1987 and 1992.[[22]](#cite_note-22) In these, he conceded only 21 goals (0.36 per game) and kept 41 clean sheets (70.69%), both averages being a record for the Italy national team. He previously featured in the Italian squad at the [1984 Olympics](/wiki/1984_Summer_Olympics "1984 Summer Olympics"), where the team managed a fourth-place finish, and also featured as one of the [Italy under-21 side](/wiki/Italy_national_under-21_football_team "Italy national under-21 football team")'s overage players at the [1986 UEFA European Under-21 Championship](/wiki/1986_UEFA_European_Under-21_Championship "1986 UEFA European Under-21 Championship"), as the team's starting goalkeeper.[[23]](#cite_note-23) He was also included in [Enzo Bearzot](/wiki/Enzo_Bearzot "Enzo Bearzot")'s 22-man Italy squad for the [1986 World Cup](/wiki/1986_FIFA_World_Cup "1986 FIFA World Cup"). Initially selected as the team's third goalkeeper behind Fiorentina's [Giovanni Galli](/wiki/Giovanni_Galli "Giovanni Galli") and Roma's [Franco Tancredi](/wiki/Franco_Tancredi "Franco Tancredi"), his name was taken in consideration by Bearzot before the match against the [Michel Platini](/wiki/Michel_Platini "Michel Platini")-led [France](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team") due to the poor performances of Galli (who, in the end, also played against France).[[11]](#cite_note-storiedicalcio-11)

Zenga became the Italy national side's starting goalkeeper under manager [Azeglio Vicini](/wiki/Azeglio_Vicini "Azeglio Vicini"), ahead of his perceived career rival, [Stefano Tacconi](/wiki/Stefano_Tacconi "Stefano Tacconi").[[7]](#cite_note-alt-7)[[11]](#cite_note-storiedicalcio-11)[[24]](#cite_note-MIA_CARA_INTER_TI_LASCIO_COSI-24)[[25]](#cite_note-rivalità-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-beffa-27) During the [1988 UEFA European Championships](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1988 "UEFA Euro 1988"), Zenga played all four of Italy's matches (a 1–1 draw against [West Germany](/wiki/Germany_national_football_team "Germany national football team"), a 1–0 victory over [Spain](/wiki/Spain_national_football_team "Spain national football team"), and a 2–0 win over [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark_national_football_team "Denmark national football team") in the group stage matches, and a 0–2 loss against the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union_national_football_team "Soviet Union national football team") in the semi-final). Here again Zenga was at the centre of controversy: in the first match against West Germany he conceded a free kick inside the penalty area due to having made too many steps while carrying the ball in his hands (an infringement rarely penalised). [Andreas Brehme](/wiki/Andreas_Brehme "Andreas Brehme"), who would become Zenga's teammate at Inter only a few months later, scored from the resulting free kick to tie the game for West Germany. Italy reached the semi-finals of the competition.[[11]](#cite_note-storiedicalcio-11)[[12]](#cite_note-itv-12)

Zenga remained first choice goalkeeper when Italy hosted the World Cup in [1990](/wiki/1990_FIFA_World_Cup "1990 FIFA World Cup"), and helped the team to a third-place finish, during which he set a record of five consecutive clean sheets, and a total of 518 minutes without conceding a goal, a record still standing.[[7]](#cite_note-alt-7)[[11]](#cite_note-storiedicalcio-11)[[28]](#cite_note-28) His unbeaten streak was ended by [Claudio Caniggia](/wiki/Claudio_Caniggia "Claudio Caniggia")'s header in the semi-finals against defending champions [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina_national_football_team "Argentina national football team"), after Zenga had made an error when coming out to collect a cross; following a 1–1 draw after extra-time, Argentina advanced to the final 4–3 on penalties, while Zenga failed to stop a single spot kick in the shoot-out.[[11]](#cite_note-storiedicalcio-11)[[29]](#cite_note-ilfoglio-29)[[27]](#cite_note-beffa-27) In the third-place match against [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team"), Zenga conceded his second goal of the tournament when he was beaten by a [David Platt](/wiki/David_Platt_(footballer) "David Platt (footballer)") header, although Italy managed to capture the bronze medal with a 2–1 victory.[[30]](#cite_note-storiedicalcio.altervista.org-30)

After Italy had failed to [qualify](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1992_qualifying "UEFA Euro 1992 qualifying") for the [1992 European Championship](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1992 "UEFA Euro 1992"), [Arrigo Sacchi](/wiki/Arrigo_Sacchi "Arrigo Sacchi") was appointed as Italy's new manager, and he eventually excluded Zenga from his side, in favour of goalkeepers who were more suited to his zonal marking defensive system, such as [Gianluca Pagliuca](/wiki/Gianluca_Pagliuca "Gianluca Pagliuca"), and [Luca Marchegiani](/wiki/Luca_Marchegiani "Luca Marchegiani").[[11]](#cite_note-storiedicalcio-11)[[31]](#cite_note-silenzio-31)[[32]](#cite_note-fuori_Zenga-32)

## Style of play

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walter_Zenga&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Style of play")]

An aggressive, consistent, physically strong, complete, and athletic goalkeeper, Zenga was nicknamed *Deltaplano* ("[Hang glider](/wiki/Hang_gliding "Hang gliding")")[[7]](#cite_note-alt-7)[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-sfida_con_Zenga-34)[[35]](#cite_note-lastampa-35) due to his excellent shot-stopping abilities, positioning, explosive reactions, bravery, and in particular for his speed, elegance, and agility, which enabled him to produce spectacular saves.[[7]](#cite_note-alt-7)[[11]](#cite_note-storiedicalcio-11)[[29]](#cite_note-ilfoglio-29)[[35]](#cite_note-lastampa-35) Despite his reputation, the media was often critical of Zenga's penalty-saving record throughout his career,[[29]](#cite_note-ilfoglio-29)[[35]](#cite_note-lastampa-35) although he stopped penalties against notable specialists, such as [Roberto Baggio](/wiki/Roberto_Baggio "Roberto Baggio"), [Paul Merson](/wiki/Paul_Merson "Paul Merson") and [Michel Platini](/wiki/Michel_Platini "Michel Platini");[[12]](#cite_note-itv-12) he was also criticised by pundits for his unsteady performances when coming out off his line to claim crosses, and performed best between the posts,[[11]](#cite_note-storiedicalcio-11)[[29]](#cite_note-ilfoglio-29)[[35]](#cite_note-lastampa-35)[[36]](#cite_note-repubblica-36) while he was also not particularly adept with the ball at his feet,[[34]](#cite_note-sfida_con_Zenga-34) or very confident in his distribution,[[37]](#cite_note-37) and initially struggled in teams which employed a zonal marking defensive system and the offside trap, due to his reluctancy to [rush out of goal](/wiki/Goalkeeper_(association_football)#Sweeper-keeper "Goalkeeper (association football)").[[31]](#cite_note-silenzio-31) Nevertheless, he was able to adapt successfully to the changes in regulations following the introduction of the back-pass rule, and maintained a high level of performance as his career progressed, even as goalkeepers were required to play more frequently with their feet.[[38]](#cite_note-38) In addition to his goalkeeping ability, Zenga also stood out for his strong mentality and leadership from the back, as well as his temper and flamboyant celebrations as a footballer,[[12]](#cite_note-itv-12)[[31]](#cite_note-silenzio-31)[[39]](#cite_note-39) and was also known for his composure under pressure, which enabled him not to be fazed if he ever made any errors.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

His other nickname, *L'Uomo Ragno* ("[Spider-Man](/wiki/Spider-Man "Spider-Man")"),[[7]](#cite_note-alt-7) is not related to his goalkeeping skills, but rather to a curious circumstance: in 1992, while answering questions about his exclusion from the Italy national team, Zenga softly sang a song by the Italian band [883](/wiki/883_(band) "883 (band)"), called *Hanno ucciso l'Uomo Ragno* ("Someone killed Spider-Man"),[[32]](#cite_note-fuori_Zenga-32) which led pundits and supporters to call him like the [Marvel Comics](/wiki/Marvel_Comics "Marvel Comics") character.

## Managerial career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walter_Zenga&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Managerial career")]

### Early career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walter_Zenga&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Early career")]

Zenga became the head coach of the [New England Revolution](/wiki/New_England_Revolution "New England Revolution") on August 24, 1998, replacing Thomas Rongen for the final six matches of the 1998 season.[[2]](#cite_note-New_England_Revolution_Club_History-2) On October 28, Zenga was announced as Player-Manager of New England Revolution heading into the 1999 season.[[41]](#cite_note-41) His tenure as Player-Manager would last less than one full campaign, as he was replaced with two matches left left to play, following the Revolution's 1–0 loss to the [Dallas Burn](/wiki/Dallas_Burn "Dallas Burn"), on September 30, 1999.[[2]](#cite_note-New_England_Revolution_Club_History-2) After he left the club, Zenga retired from active football, choosing to pursue a coaching career.

After a short stint with Milanese [Serie D](/wiki/Serie_D "Serie D") team [Brera](/wiki/Brera_Calcio "Brera Calcio"), Zenga moved to Romania in 2002, first managing [Naţional București](/wiki/AFC_Progresul_Bucure%C8%99ti "AFC Progresul București") and then [Steaua București](/wiki/FCSB "FCSB"), where he won the [domestic title](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I") and reached the Round of 16 of the [2004–05 UEFA Cup](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_UEFA_Cup "2004–05 UEFA Cup") after eliminating UEFA Cup winners [Valencia](/wiki/Valencia_CF "Valencia CF") from the competition.

In the summer 2005, after being fired from Steaua București before the end of the season, Zenga joined [Red Star Belgrade](/wiki/Red_Star_Belgrade "Red Star Belgrade"), leading the Serbo-Montenegrin team to a [double](/wiki/Double_(association_football) "Double (association football)") ([national league](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_Serbia_and_Montenegro_SuperLiga "2005–06 Serbia and Montenegro SuperLiga") and [national cup](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_Serbia_and_Montenegro_Cup "2005–06 Serbia and Montenegro Cup")).

In the summer 2006, Zenga was appointed coach of Turkish [Süper Lig](/wiki/S%C3%BCper_Lig "Süper Lig") side [Gaziantepspor](/wiki/Gaziantepspor "Gaziantepspor"); however, after a poor start (five wins in 17 league matches), he resigned in January 2007 in order to accept an offer from [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates") club [Al-Ain](/wiki/Al_Ain_Club "Al Ain Club").

After just five months in charge, Al-Ain sacked Zenga, who was announced in September 2007 as new [Dinamo București](/wiki/FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Dinamo București") coach, replacing [Mircea Rednic](/wiki/Mircea_Rednic "Mircea Rednic"), but he resigned only two months later following a 1–0 loss in a local derby lost to Steaua.[[42]](#cite_note-42) He then accepted a job as a football commentator and pundit for Italian public broadcasting service [RAI](/wiki/RAI "RAI").

### Catania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walter_Zenga&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Catania")]

On 1 April 2008, he agreed to replace resigning boss [Silvio Baldini](/wiki/Silvio_Baldini "Silvio Baldini") as manager of [Catania](/wiki/Calcio_Catania "Calcio Catania").[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-zenga_catania-44) He made his Serie A debut on 6 April with a 3–0 home win against [Napoli](/wiki/S.S.C._Napoli "S.S.C. Napoli"),[[45]](#cite_note-45) leading them to a dramatic relegation escape during the final minutes of the league, after a 1–1 home draw against [Roma](/wiki/A.S._Roma "A.S. Roma").

Confirmed at the helm of Catania for the [2008–09](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_Serie_A "2008–09 Serie A") season, Zenga proved to be fit for the Italian top flight, leading the *rossoazzurri* to impressive results in the early part of the season, and agreeing a one-year contract extension with the Sicilian club.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

Catania's playing style under Zenga was notable for the coach's focus on free kick planning; his assistant manager Gianni Vio is known to work exclusively on this particular side of football tactics during the weekly training sessions.[[47]](#cite_note-47) He guided Catania to a mid-table finish and the Serie A points record for the eastern Sicilian side; at the final home game of the season he announced he was parting company with his club by mutual consent.

### Palermo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walter_Zenga&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Palermo")]

On 5 June 2009, after being linked with the managerial job at [Lazio](/wiki/S.S._Lazio "S.S. Lazio") it was revealed that Zenga had agreed a three-year contract with [Palermo](/wiki/U.S._Citt%C3%A0_di_Palermo "U.S. Città di Palermo") to replace outgoing manager [Davide Ballardini](/wiki/Davide_Ballardini "Davide Ballardini"); the move was seen as a massive surprise due to the *Rosanero* club being rumoured to be interested in several other managers and the bitter rivalry between them and Catania, the only two Sicilian teams playing in the Italian top flight.[[48]](#cite_note-48) He debuted with a 4–2 [Coppa Italia](/wiki/Coppa_Italia "Coppa Italia") win over [SPAL 1907](/wiki/SPAL_1907 "SPAL 1907"), and a 2–1 home win against [Napoli](/wiki/S.S.C._Napoli "S.S.C. Napoli") in the first week of the Serie A season. However, a number of disappointing results followed, ending in an unimpressive 1–1 home tie to Catania that led Palermo chairman [Maurizio Zamparini](/wiki/Maurizio_Zamparini "Maurizio Zamparini") to remove Zenga from his managerial duties on 23 November, after only thirteen league games in charge of the *rosanero*.[[49]](#cite_note-zenga_sacked-49)

### Middle East

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walter_Zenga&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Middle East")]

On 11 May 2010, he was announced new head coach of [Saudi Professional League](/wiki/Saudi_Professional_League "Saudi Professional League") club [Al-Nassr](/wiki/Al-Nassr "Al-Nassr").[[50]](#cite_note-50) He was removed from his position on 24 December after a string of poor results led Al-Nassr to be overtaken at the top of the league table.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

On 6 January 2011, Zenga was appointed new head coach of [Al Nasr](/wiki/Al_Nasr_SC_(Dubai) "Al Nasr SC (Dubai)") in the [UAE Pro-League](/wiki/UAE_Pro-League "UAE Pro-League").[[52]](#cite_note-52)

### Return to Italy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walter_Zenga&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Return to Italy")]

On 4 June 2015, Zenga returned to Italy, and was appointed head coach at [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A") side [Sampdoria](/wiki/U.C._Sampdoria "U.C. Sampdoria") for the 2015–16 season.[[53]](#cite_note-53) However, after he was sacked in November, and replaced by [Vincenzo Montella](/wiki/Vincenzo_Montella "Vincenzo Montella") as head coach,[[54]](#cite_note-54) he later returned to the Middle East to manage bottom placed club [Al-Shaab](/wiki/Al-Shaab_CSC "Al-Shaab CSC"), however he was unable to turn around the club's fortunes and left the club on 20 February 2016 by mutual consent.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

### Wolverhampton Wanderers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walter_Zenga&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Wolverhampton Wanderers")]

On 30 July 2016, Zenga was appointed head coach of [Football League Championship](/wiki/Football_League_Championship "Football League Championship") side [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers "Wolverhampton Wanderers") for the 2016–17 season.[[56]](#cite_note-56) Despite having never managed in England, he cited his vast foreign experience as enough to succeed.[[57]](#cite_note-57) On 2 August, he made his first signings, buying Icelandic striker [Jón Daði Böðvarsson](/wiki/J%C3%B3n_Da%C3%B0i_B%C3%B6%C3%B0varsson "Jón Daði Böðvarsson") from [Kaiserslautern](/wiki/1._FC_Kaiserslautern "1. FC Kaiserslautern") and bringing in Portuguese midfielder [João Teixeira](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_Teixeira_(footballer,_born_1994) "João Teixeira (footballer, born 1994)") on a season-long loan from [Benfica](/wiki/S.L._Benfica "S.L. Benfica").[[58]](#cite_note-58)

In his first game on 6 August, Zenga's team drew 2–2 away to [Rotherham United](/wiki/Rotherham_United_F.C. "Rotherham United F.C."), coming back from a 2–0 deficit with ten players;[[59]](#cite_note-59) he described his first game as an "amazing experience".[[60]](#cite_note-60) Zenga took Wolves on a six-game unbeaten run in all competitions, and eight points from his first four league games, including a 3–1 win at local rivals [Birmingham City](/wiki/Birmingham_City_F.C. "Birmingham City F.C.").[[61]](#cite_note-61) He praised the Wolves players' spirit and credited them with leading them to the unbeaten start.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

Following the end of the summer transfer window, Zenga, having made ten new signings since his appointment, claimed that the Wolves squad was so strong that he could field two different teams if needed.[[63]](#cite_note-63) Following a 4–0 loss to [Barnsley](/wiki/Barnsley_F.C. "Barnsley F.C."), Wolves beat promotion favourites [Newcastle United](/wiki/Newcastle_United_F.C. "Newcastle United F.C."), to end their five-game winning run.[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65) Zenga claimed his team's subsequent performances showed that the defeat to Barnsley was "an accident".[[66]](#cite_note-66) Wolves then went on a five-game winless run, losing four, that would lead to his dismissal.

Despite the insistence of [Dave Edwards](/wiki/David_Edwards_(footballer,_born_1986) "David Edwards (footballer, born 1986)") that Zenga retained the support of the Wolves players, he was dismissed on 25 October following only 4 wins out of the club's first 14 Championship fixtures and Wolves 18th in the table.[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68) On 7 April 2017, Wolves director Jeff Shi said: "I liked Walter. He was passionate, a really lovely guy... Later we found out it was not a good match. We had to change very quickly... The only big mistake in my mind was the coach appointment at the beginning of the season."[[69]](#cite_note-69)

### Return to Italy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walter_Zenga&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Return to Italy")]

On 8 December 2017, Zenga was appointed [Crotone](/wiki/F.C._Crotone "F.C. Crotone") manager.[[70]](#cite_note-70) The team was relegated to Serie B at the end of the year.

Zenga returned into management on 11 October 2018 as he was named new head coach of Serie B club [Venezia](/wiki/Venezia_F.C. "Venezia F.C.") in place of [Stefano Vecchi](/wiki/Stefano_Vecchi "Stefano Vecchi").[[71]](#cite_note-zenga_venezia-71) He was sacked on 5 March 2019, after a run of four losses in five games had the club fighting relegation.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

On 3 March 2020, Zenga was appointed new head coach of Serie A club [Cagliari](/wiki/Cagliari_Calcio "Cagliari Calcio"), signing a contract until 30 June 2021, with Under-19 coach Max Canzi named as his assistant.[[73]](#cite_note-73) His debut for the [Sardinian](/wiki/Sardinia "Sardinia") club was delayed due to the [coronavirus pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Italy "COVID-19 pandemic in Italy") and subsequent [nationwide lockdown](/wiki/2020_Italy_coronavirus_lockdown "2020 Italy coronavirus lockdown").[[74]](#cite_note-74) He won three and drew four of his 13 games in charge as the team finished 14th, and was replaced by [Eusebio Di Francesco](/wiki/Eusebio_Di_Francesco "Eusebio Di Francesco") in August.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

On 27 September 2024, Italian Serie D club [Siracusa](/wiki/ASD_Siracusa_Calcio_1924 "ASD Siracusa Calcio 1924") announced the hiring of Zenga as a "club manager" and "brand ambassador", in a non-coaching role within the club.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

## Style of management

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walter_Zenga&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Style of management")]

As a manager, Zenga usually uses a four–man back-line with his teams, while he has used several different tactical systems and formations in midfield and attack throughout his career.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walter_Zenga&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Zenga has been married three times; he has five children.

### Marriages and relationships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walter_Zenga&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Marriages and relationships")]

In 1982, young professional goalkeeper Zenga—playing on loan in [San Benedetto del Tronto](/wiki/San_Benedetto_del_Tronto "San Benedetto del Tronto") with Serie B side [U.S. Sambenedettese](/wiki/U.S._Sambenedettese "U.S. Sambenedettese")—married the local [model](/wiki/Model_(person) "Model (person)") and 1980 Miss [Marche](/wiki/Marche "Marche") Elvira Carfagna, having first met her at Palazzina Azzurra on the [town's waterfront](/wiki/San_Benedetto_del_Tronto_Waterfront "San Benedetto del Tronto Waterfront").[[78]](#cite_note-78)[[79]](#cite_note-79) As Zenga re-joined Inter, the couple moved to [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"). During his marriage to Carfagna, Zenga had an [extramarital affair](/wiki/Extramarital_affair "Extramarital affair") with television presenter [Marina Perzy](/wiki/Marina_Perzy "Marina Perzy") throughout 1985.[[80]](#cite_note-larepubblica-feb1988-80) Already subject of media rumours, the ongoing affair generated public interest that culminated in February 1985 during an [international break](/wiki/1984%E2%80%9385_Inter_Milan_season "1984–85 Inter Milan season") when Inter goalkeeper Zenga could not be reached to accept a national team call-up for a friendly match as [part](/wiki/Italy_national_football_team_results_(1970%E2%80%931989)#1985 "Italy national football team results (1970–1989)") of Italy's FIFA World Cup preparation. After Italian Football Federation officials' failure to get a hold of the young goalkeeper through his wife who didn't know where he was, it was discovered that Zenga was on a romantic weekend getaway with his [mistress](/wiki/Mistress_(lover) "Mistress (lover)") Perzy.[[81]](#cite_note-vlad-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82) The event reportedly even prompted Inter president and owner [Ernesto Pellegrini](/wiki/Ernesto_Pellegrini "Ernesto Pellegrini") to privately intervene with both Zenga and Perzy.[[81]](#cite_note-vlad-81) In November 1986, with his affair with Perzy behind him, Zenga had a child, son Jacopo, with his wife Carfagna. By late 1987, he became romantically involved with another television personality, [Roberta Termali](/w/index.php?title=Roberta_Termali&action=edit&redlink=1 "Roberta Termali (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roberta_Termali "it:Roberta Termali")], whom he had been co-hosting a weekly Friday evening program, *[Forza Italia](/w/index.php?title=Forza_Italia_(television_program)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Forza Italia (television program) (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Forza_Italia_(programma_televisivo) "it:Forza Italia (programma televisivo)")]*, with since September 1987 on [Odeon TV](/wiki/Odeon_24 "Odeon 24").[[83]](#cite_note-83)[[84]](#cite_note-84) The relationship led to Zenga leaving his family and moving in with Termali in January 1988.[[80]](#cite_note-larepubblica-feb1988-80) Decades later, in February 2021, while appearing as guest on [Barbara D'Urso](/wiki/Barbara_D%27Urso "Barbara D'Urso")'s [talk/variety show](/w/index.php?title=Live_-_Non_%C3%A8_la_d%27Urso&action=edit&redlink=1 "Live - Non è la d'Urso (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Live_-_Non_%C3%A8_la_d%27Urso "it:Live - Non è la d'Urso")] on [Canale 5](/wiki/Canale_5 "Canale 5"), Carfagna publicly accused Termali of "stealing her husband" and "pretending to be her friend in order to get to [Zenga]".[[85]](#cite_note-85)[[86]](#cite_note-86)[[87]](#cite_note-87)

Two years into Zenga's relationship with Termali, the couple's first child, son Nicolò, was born out-of-wedlock in December 1989. Zenga and Termali wed in September 1992 in a notable ceremony at the Via Palestra municipal gardens in Milan with Zenga's Inter teammate [Davide Fontolan](/wiki/Davide_Fontolan "Davide Fontolan") as the groom's best man and a prominent guest list that included Milan mayor [Giampiero Borghini](/wiki/Giampiero_Borghini "Giampiero Borghini"), Inter head coach [Osvaldo Bagnoli](/wiki/Osvaldo_Bagnoli "Osvaldo Bagnoli"), Inter vice-chairman [Peppino Prisco](/wiki/Giuseppe_Prisco "Giuseppe Prisco"), Italy national team manager [Azeglio Vicini](/wiki/Azeglio_Vicini "Azeglio Vicini") as well as footballers such as [Gianluca Vialli](/wiki/Gianluca_Vialli "Gianluca Vialli") and [Ruud Gullit](/wiki/Ruud_Gullit "Ruud Gullit").[[88]](#cite_note-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89)[[90]](#cite_note-90)[[91]](#cite_note-91) Their second child, son Andrea, was born in September 1993.[[92]](#cite_note-92) The couple divorced in 1997.

After divorcing Termali, now veteran professional footballer Zenga entered a relationship with television presenter [Hoara Borselli](/w/index.php?title=Hoara_Borselli&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hoara Borselli (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hoara_Borselli "it:Hoara Borselli")], sixteen years his junior. With Zenga winding down his playing career in the United States, the couple lived in the country as he began transitioning into football coaching. Still unsure as to which line of work he ultimately wanted to pursue after playing professional football, Zenga also took media jobs in Italy, often with his new partner. The couple ended up spending six years together during which they notably co-hosted [home shopping](/wiki/Home_shopping "Home shopping") programs on privately owned television channels in Italy, selling appliances and [consumer goods](/wiki/Consumer_goods "Consumer goods") such as [vacuum cleaners](/wiki/Vacuum_cleaners "Vacuum cleaners") and [electro-stimulators](/wiki/Electrical_muscle_stimulation "Electrical muscle stimulation").[[93]](#cite_note-93)

In May 2005 while coaching in [Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest"), Zenga married 23-year-old [Romanian](/wiki/Romania "Romania") woman Raluca Rebedea, 22 years his junior.[[94]](#cite_note-gazzetta0408-94) Three years later, in June 2008, the couple organized another wedding ceremony while on vacation in [Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas "Las Vegas"); held in a [Vegas chapel](/wiki/List_of_wedding_chapels_in_Las_Vegas "List of wedding chapels in Las Vegas"), Zenga posted the [wedding](/wiki/Las_Vegas_weddings "Las Vegas weddings") footage on his YouTube channel.[[95]](#cite_note-95) On 19 November 2009, Rebedea gave birth to their daughter Samira Valentina.[[96]](#cite_note-96) Though the family resided in [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai "Dubai"), in April 2010, Zenga stated his willingness to take [Romanian citizenship](/wiki/Romanian_nationality_law "Romanian nationality law"),[[97]](#cite_note-97) which he reportedly obtained in April 2012.[[98]](#cite_note-98) The couple's second child, son Walter Jr., was born in 2012.[[99]](#cite_note-99) In October 2020, Zenga made the dissolution of his and Rebedea's 14-year marriage public via a lengthy [Instagram](/wiki/Instagram "Instagram") post, revealing that the two "hadn't been a couple for some time" and identifying the reasons for the split in "both the distance, created because of [Zenga's] work as a [football] coach, as well as due to [Rebedea's] radical change", adding he had already filed for divorce at the Dubai Court of Appeal before concluding that "the freedom I've always given her has somehow turned against us [as a couple]".[[100]](#cite_note-larepubblica-100)[[101]](#cite_note-101) Zenga's particular statement about "the freedom he had given to his wife" triggered a reaction by Italian journalist [Selvaggia Lucarelli](/w/index.php?title=Selvaggia_Lucarelli&action=edit&redlink=1 "Selvaggia Lucarelli (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Selvaggia_Lucarelli "it:Selvaggia Lucarelli")] who criticized the former goalkeeper for "considering his wife's freedom a concession".[[100]](#cite_note-larepubblica-100) The resulting controversy online in Italy led to Zenga removing the post.[[100]](#cite_note-larepubblica-100)

### Children

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Walter_Zenga&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Children")]

Zenga's son with Carfagna, Jacopo Zenga, became a professional footballer himself, playing in [Serie D](/wiki/Serie_D "Serie D") after spending time with Inter and [Genoa](/wiki/Genoa_C.F.C. "Genoa C.F.C.") at youth level.

The younger of Zenga's two sons with Termali, Andrea Zenga, also pursued professional football for a few years before turning to modelling and celebrity [reality television](/wiki/Reality_television "Reality television") in Italy. In September 2018, he appeared with his fiancé Alessandra Sgolastra on the [first season](/w/index.php?title=Temptation_Island_VIP_(Italy,_first_season)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Temptation Island VIP (Italy, first season) (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Temptation_Island_VIP_(prima_edizione) "it:Temptation Island VIP (prima edizione)")] of *[Italian Celebrity Temptation Island](/w/index.php?title=Temptation_Island_VIP&action=edit&redlink=1 "Temptation Island VIP (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Temptation_Island_VIP "it:Temptation Island VIP")]* on [Canale 5](/wiki/Canale_5 "Canale 5") as well as on the [fifth season](/wiki/Grande_Fratello_VIP_season_5 "Grande Fratello VIP season 5") of *[Grande Fratello VIP](/wiki/Grande_Fratello_VIP "Grande Fratello VIP")* (Italian Celebrity Big Brother) in December 2020. The *Grande Fratello VIP* appearance was marked by his opening up about harbouring ill feelings regarding his father Walter's alleged absentee parenting, a claim that received a lot of press in Italy including reaction from the parties involved.[[102]](#cite_note-102)[[103]](#cite_note-103)[[104]](#cite_note-104)
